questions.promptTex: \text{What is the complement of an } 28° \text{ angle?}
questions.explanation: $90° - 28° = 62°$. The two together form a right angle.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the supplement of a } 130° \text{ angle?}
questions.explanation: $180° - 130° = 50°$. Together they form a straight line.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two lines intersect, forming an angle of } 75°. \\ \text{What is the measure of the vertical angle?}
hints: Vertical angles are formed by the intersection.
questions.explanation: Vertical angles (the angles directly across from each other when two lines cross) are always equal. So the vertical angle is also **75°**.
$180° < 250° < 360°$ → **reflex**. Reflex angles 'wrap around' more than half a revolution.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two angles are supplementary. One is } 47°. \\ \text{What is the other?}
steps: Supplementary means the two angles add to $180°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two angles are complementary. One is } 38°. \\ \text{What is the other?}
steps: Complementary means the two angles add to $90°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{An angle and its complement sum to } 90°. \\ \text{The complement is } 4 \text{ times the angle. Find the angle.}
steps: Let the angle be $x$. The complement is $4x$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two angles form a linear pair. One is } 63°. \\ \text{What is the other?}
steps: A linear pair forms a straight line — together they sum to $180°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two intersecting lines form vertical angles. One vertical angle is} \\ (3x + 10)° \text{ and its vertical partner is } (5x - 30)°. \\ \text{Find } x.
steps: Vertical angles (formed by intersecting lines) are **equal**.

questions.promptTex: \text{An angle is bisected into two } 27° \text{ pieces.} \\ \text{What is the original angle?}
hints: Bisecting splits an angle into two EQUAL halves.
steps: An angle bisector divides an angle into two equal parts.
Each half is $27°$, so the original is $2 \cdot 27° = 54°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Three angles around a point sum to } 360°. \\ \text{Two are } 110° \text{ and } 145°. \text{ Find the third.}
hints: All angles around a point sum to $360°$.
steps: Angles around a single point sum to $360°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which pair of angles must be congruent?}
hints: Vertical angles are formed by two intersecting lines.
They're across from each other and always equal.
questions.explanation: Vertical angles are always equal. The other pairs are equal only in special cases.
steps: Vertical angles (formed by two intersecting lines, opposite each other) are **always congruent** — that's a theorem.
Complementary, supplementary, and adjacent are pairs defined by their sum or position; they're not equal in general.
